title: Your nature vs your imbalance (prakriti vs vikriti)
excerpt: You were born with a unique constitutional blueprint, your prakriti. But everyday stress, poor sleep, and wrong foods can shift you into vikriti, a state of imbalance. Knowing the difference is the first step in Ayurveda's approach to genuine wellbeing.
at_a_glance: Prakriti is your fixed constitutional baseline set at conception; vikriti is your current state of imbalance.
Symptoms belong to vikriti, not to your essential nature, so you can reduce them by closing the gap.
Regular meals, consistent sleep, and season-appropriate habits are the simplest daily tools for keeping vikriti minimal.

body.heading: the you that never changes, and the you that has
paragraphs: You may have noticed that your childhood friend could eat fried food without any issue, sleep four hours and feel fresh, and rarely fall sick, while you always needed eight hours, a warm meal, and a quiet room. This is not just personality or luck. Ayurveda says you each were born with a different prakriti, a Sanskrit word that simply means 'your original nature' or constitution. It is the particular combination of the three doshas, vata, pitta, and kapha, that was set at the moment of conception and remains your baseline for life.

The Caraka Samhita, one of the oldest Ayurvedic texts, explains prakriti as the inherent proportion of the doshas determined by the state of the parents, the season, the food of the mother, and several other factors at the time of conception (Caraka Samhita, Sharirasthana). This is not a vague philosophical idea. It has real, observable effects: the texture of your skin, the pace of your digestion, how you respond to cold weather, whether you tend toward anxiety or lethargy. These traits stay fairly stable across your life. They are your prakriti.
body.pullQuote: You are not broken. You have simply drifted.

body.heading: vikriti: when life pulls you away from yourself
paragraphs: Then there is vikriti, meaning 'deviation from nature' or, more plainly, your current state of imbalance. While prakriti is your born template, vikriti is what happens when diet, stress, season, sleep habits, or emotional strain push one or more doshas out of their natural proportion. A person with a pitta-dominant prakriti, for instance, may develop excess pitta in the form of acid reflux, skin rashes, or sharp irritability, especially in summer or during a demanding work period.

The Ashtanga Hridaya, a concise classical text compiled by Vagbhata, describes vikriti as the state where the doshas are vitiated and have moved away from their natural seats, causing disease (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). The Sushruta Samhita similarly notes that when the doshas deviate from their natural balance, they become the root cause of illness (Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana). This means the goal of an Ayurvedic consultation is never simply to identify your dosha type. It is to understand the gap between your prakriti and your present vikriti, and then to gently reduce that gap.

The important point is that vikriti can affect doshas that are not even dominant in your prakriti. A kapha person can develop vata imbalance through too much travel, irregular meals, or prolonged grief. Recognising that the symptoms you are experiencing belong to vikriti, not to your essential nature, can itself be reassuring. You are not broken. You have simply drifted.

body.heading: everyday ways to know where you stand
paragraphs: Prakriti is best assessed by a trained vaidya through direct observation: pulse, skin, eyes, tongue, and detailed history. But you can develop a working awareness on your own. Think about your consistent long-term tendencies, the way your digestion has always behaved, whether you have always run warm or cool, how you have historically slept. Those stable patterns point toward prakriti.

Vikriti, on the other hand, shows up as recent changes: a skin that was always clear is now breaking out, a digestion that was usually strong is now sluggish, a temperament that was generally patient is lately short-fused. The Caraka Samhita instructs the physician to compare the patient's current state with their natural baseline before recommending any intervention (Caraka Samhita, Vimanasthana). This comparative view is the practical heart of Ayurvedic diagnosis.

Simple daily habits can help keep vikriti from deepening. Eating at regular times suits all constitutions because it supports agni, the digestive fire. Sleeping and waking at consistent hours calms vata. Avoiding excessive heat, spicy food, and midday sun in summer prevents pitta from flaring. Keeping the body moving with gentle daily activity prevents kapha from accumulating. These are not cures. They are the kind of ordinary maintenance that keeps the gap between prakriti and vikriti small.

body.heading: when to see a vaidya or doctor
paragraphs: General information about prakriti and vikriti is a useful orientation, not a diagnosis or a substitute for professional care. If you are experiencing persistent symptoms such as chronic fatigue, ongoing digestive trouble, skin conditions that do not resolve, frequent illness, or significant mood disturbance, please consult a qualified Ayurvedic practitioner or a medical doctor.

Certain signs need prompt medical attention regardless of your constitution: unexplained weight loss, blood in the stool or urine, chest pain, difficulty breathing, high fever, or any symptom that comes on suddenly and severely. Ayurveda works best as part of an informed, integrated approach to health. A proper prakriti and vikriti assessment from a trained vaidya will give you far more accurate and personalised guidance than any self-evaluation can.

try_this_today.title: Map your stable traits
try_this_today.description: Write down five health tendencies you have had since childhood: digestion, sleep depth, skin type, tolerance of heat or cold, and typical mood under stress. These stable patterns are clues to your prakriti.

try_this_today.title: Spot the recent changes
try_this_today.description: Note anything that has shifted in the last few weeks or months: energy, appetite, skin, sleep quality, temperament. Recent changes point to vikriti and are the ones worth addressing first.

try_this_today.title: Eat at fixed times
try_this_today.description: Commit to three meals at roughly the same time each day. This simple habit supports agni across all constitutions and is one of the first recommendations in Ayurvedic daily routine (dinacharya).

try_this_today.title: Adjust for the season
try_this_today.description: In summer, reduce spicy and oily food and prefer cooling foods like cucumber, coriander water, and light grains. In winter, add warmth with ginger tea and heavier nourishing meals. Season-dosha mismatch is a common trigger for vikriti.

words_to_know.definition: Your original constitutional nature, the stable dosha proportion set at conception.
Deviation from your natural state; the current imbalance of doshas caused by diet, stress, or lifestyle.
One of three functional energies (vata, pitta, kapha) that govern all physiological and psychological processes.
Digestive fire; the transformative capacity that drives digestion, metabolism, and mental clarity.
Daily routine; the Ayurvedic framework of consistent habits aligned with natural rhythms.
A trained Ayurvedic physician or practitioner.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sharirasthana (on the determination of prakriti) · Caraka Samhita, Vimanasthana (on comparing current state with natural baseline) · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana (on vitiation of doshas and disease) · Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana (on doshas deviating from natural balance)
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
